Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides throughout the movement. Avoid swinging your back to lift the weight. Control the descent.
Pose.Name: Dumbbell Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement, biceps tear

steps.step: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing forward. Keep elbows close to your sides.
Exhale as you curl the dumbbells toward your shoulders, contracting your biceps. Keep your upper arms stationary.
Pause briefly at the top, squeezing your biceps. Do not let your shoulders roll forward.
Inhale as you slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with control. Perform 2 sets of 10 reps.
If you can not keep your elbows still, the weight is too heavy. Focus on the negative (lowering) phase for better growth.

common_mistakes.mistake: Swinging the back to generate momentum.
Moving the elbows forward during the curl.
Using wrists to curl instead of biceps.
Dropping the weight quickly on the descent.

Pose.safety: Keep your wrists neutral (straight) throughout the movement. Avoid rotating your palms - the hammer grip is fixed. Control the movement.
Pose.Name: Dumbbells Hammer Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, forearm strain

steps.step: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides with palms facing each other (neutral grip). Keep elbows pinned to your sides.
Exhale as you curl the dumbbells toward your shoulders, maintaining the neutral palm position throughout.
Pause at the top, feeling the contraction in your brachialis (outer forearm).
Inhale as you lower with control to the starting position. Perform 2 sets of 10 reps.
This exercise builds thickness in the upper arm. Keep your wrists straight - do not let them bend backward or forward.

common_mistakes.mistake: Rotating palms upward during the curl.
Letting elbows flare out from the body.
Using shoulder momentum to lift.
Wrist flexion instead of maintaining neutral.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides. Avoid letting your elbows flare outward. Control the weight on both the push and the return.
Pose.Name: Tricep Pushdown (Cable/Band)
Pose.Equipment: Cable Machine or Resistance Band, Rope or Bar Attachment
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Triceps Brachii (All 3 heads - Long, Lateral, Medial)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Attach a rope or straight bar to a high pulley. Grip the attachment with palms facing down, elbows at 90 degrees, tucked to sides.
Exhale as you extend your arms downward until they are straight, keeping elbows stationary.
Pause briefly at the bottom, squeezing your triceps.
Inhale as you slowly return to the starting position with control. Perform 2 sets of 10 reps.
Only your forearms should move - your upper arms remain vertical and still throughout the movement.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward or move forward.
Using body weight to lean into the push.
Rushing the return phase (eccentric).
Shrugging shoulders toward the ears.

Pose.safety: Keep your shoulders down and back. Lower yourself only as far as your shoulders comfortably allow. Avoid letting your elbows flare outward.
Pose.Name: Bench Dip
Pose.Equipment: Bench or Chair, Optional: Weight on Lap
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff injury, wrist injury, elbow tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on the edge of a bench. Place hands shoulder-width apart on the bench, fingers forward. Walk feet forward, supporting your weight with straight arms.
Inhale as you slowly lower your body by bending your elbows to 90 degrees. Keep back close to the bench.
Exhale as you push through your palms to straighten your arms, returning to start.
Perform 2 sets of 10 reps. Keep shoulders down and away from ears.
The closer your feet are to your body, the easier the dip. For more challenge, extend legs further forward or add weight to your lap.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting shoulders hunch up toward the ears.
Lowering too deep (past 90 degrees) causing shoulder strain.
Elbows flaring outward instead of tracking back.
Using leg assistance to push up.

Pose.safety: Keep your core engaged and back straight. Avoid arching your lower back. Do not lock out your elbows at the top.
Pose.Name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Anterior and Medial Heads), Triceps, Trapezius
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, AC joint injury, neck strain

steps.step: Sit on a bench with back support. Hold dumbbells at shoulder height, palms facing forward, elbows bent at 90 degrees.
Exhale as you press the dumbbells directly overhead until arms are extended (but not locked).
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale as you lower with control back to shoulder height.
Perform 2 sets of 10 reps. Keep your core braced to protect your lower back.
Do not let the dumbbells touch at the top - keep them shoulder-width apart to maintain tension on the deltoids.

common_mistakes.mistake: Arching the lower back to help lift the weight.
Bringing dumbbells too close together at the top.
Flaring elbows outward excessively.
Using momentum instead of controlled pressing.

Pose.safety: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement. Avoid using momentum to swing the weights up. Control the descent.
Pose.Name: Lateral Raise
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff injury, shoulder impingement, neck strain

steps.step: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides, palms facing your body. Slight bend in elbows, shoulders down and back.
Exhale as you raise the dumbbells out to your sides, leading with your elbows. Stop when arms are parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly, then inhale as you lower with control back to starting position.
Perform 2 sets of 10 reps. Do not let your shoulders shrug up toward your ears.
Use lighter weight than you think you need. The medial deltoid is a small muscle that responds best to higher reps and strict form.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the weights up.
Raising arms above shoulder height.
Shrugging shoulders toward the ears.
Locking elbows straight.

Pose.safety: Rest your forearms fully on your thighs for stability. Move only your wrists - keep your arms and body still. Avoid jerky movements.
Pose.Name: Wrist Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Wrist tendinitis, carpal tunnel syndrome, elbow tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with wrists hanging over your knees, palms facing up. Hold a barbell or dumbbells.
Exhale as you curl your wrists upward, bringing the weight toward your forearms.
Pause at the top, squeezing your forearm flexors.
Inhale as you lower with control back to starting position. Perform 2 sets of 12 reps.
Focus on full range of motion. Let your wrists extend fully at the bottom before curling back up.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the arms instead of isolating the wrists.
Using momentum to swing the weight.
Letting wrists hyperextend at the bottom.
Holding the breath during the curl.

Pose.safety: Keep your forearms fully supported. Move only your wrists. Avoid jerking the weight - control is key.
Pose.Name: Reverse Wrist Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Wrist tendinitis, tennis elbow, carpal tunnel syndrome

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with wrists hanging over knees, palms facing down. Hold a barbell or dumbbells.
Exhale as you extend your wrists upward, raising the back of your hands toward the ceiling.
Pause at the top, feeling the contraction in your forearm extensors.
Inhale as you lower with control back to starting position. Perform 2 sets of 12 reps.
This exercise is often harder than regular wrist curls. Start with very light weight - even just the empty bar - to master the movement.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the entire arm instead of just the wrist.
Using too much weight, causing jerky movement.
Letting wrists drop too low at the bottom.
Failing to control the descent.

Pose.safety: Avoid using your lower back to swing the weight. Keep your core braced and elbows pinned to your sides. Control the barbell throughout.
Pose.Name: Barbell Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, lower back strain, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Stand holding a barbell with an underhand grip (palms up), hands shoulder-width apart. Elbows pinned to sides.
Exhale as you curl the barbell toward your shoulders, contracting your biceps. Keep upper arms stationary.
Pause at the top, squeezing your biceps hard.
Inhale as you lower with control to starting position. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.
A wider grip targets the inner biceps (short head); a narrower grip targets the outer biceps (long head). Experiment to find what works for you.

common_mistakes.mistake: Swinging the back to lift the weight (cheating curl).
Letting elbows drift forward.
Using wrist flexion instead of elbow flexion.
Dropping the weight quickly on the descent.

Pose.safety: Keep your shoulders and upper back pressed firmly against the bench. Avoid using momentum to swing the dumbbells.
Pose.Name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Biceps Brachii (Long Head - Outer Biceps), Brachialis
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, shoulder impingement, lower back issues

steps.step: Set an adjustable bench to 45-60 degrees. Lie back with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging straight down, palms facing forward.
Exhale as you curl both dumbbells toward your shoulders, keeping elbows stationary and back against the bench.
Pause at the top, squeezing your biceps.
Inhale as you lower with control to the starting position. Perform 2 sets of 12 reps.
The incline position stretches the biceps more than standing curls, which increases muscle activation. Do not let your shoulders roll forward.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting shoulders lift off the bench.
Using momentum to swing the dumbbells up.
Elbows moving forward during the curl.
Lowering arms too quickly.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pointing toward the ceiling throughout. Avoid flaring elbows outward. Control the weight on the descent.
Pose.Name: Skull Crushers (Lying Tricep Extension)
Pose.Equipment: EZ-Bar or Barbell with weight plates, Dumbbells, Bench
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Triceps Brachii (All 3 Heads, especially Long Head)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Lie on a bench holding an EZ-bar with an overhand grip. Extend arms straight up over your chest, shoulder-width apart.
Inhale as you bend your elbows, lowering the bar toward your forehead (or behind your head). Keep elbows pointing up.
Exhale as you extend your arms back to the starting position, squeezing your triceps.
Perform 3 sets of 10 reps. Keep your upper arms perpendicular to the floor throughout.
Stop when your forearms are parallel to the floor. Do not lower the bar past your head.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward.
Lowering the bar past your head (too deep).
Arching the lower back to help lift.
Using momentum to extend the arms.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides. Avoid leaning forward or using body momentum. Control the weight throughout.
Pose.Name: Cable Pushdown
Pose.Equipment: Cable Machine with V-Bar or Rope
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Attach a V-bar or rope to a high pulley. Grip with palms facing down. Stand close to the cable, elbows at 90 degrees and tucked to sides.
Exhale as you push the attachment down until your arms are fully extended.
If using a rope, split your hands apart at the bottom for a better contraction.
Inhale as you slowly return to the starting position. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps.
At the bottom of the movement, think about pushing your palms toward the floor to fully contract the triceps.

common_mistakes.mistake: Leaning forward and using body weight to push.
Letting elbows flare away from the body.
Rushing the return (eccentric) phase.
Shrugging shoulders toward the ears.

Pose.safety: Lower yourself only as far as your shoulders comfortably allow. Keep your body close to the bars. Avoid letting your shoulders roll forward.
Pose.Name: Tricep Dips
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Triceps Brachii (All 3 Heads), Chest (Minor), Anterior Deltoid
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff injury, elbow tendinitis, wrist injury

steps.step: Grip parallel bars with palms facing inward. Jump or step up to support your weight with arms straight, shoulders down.
Inhale as you slowly lower your body by bending your elbows to 90 degrees. Keep torso upright (lean forward for chest focus, upright for triceps).
Exhale as you push through your palms to straighten your arms, returning to start.
Perform 2 sets of 10 reps. Keep your core engaged throughout.
For triceps focus, keep your body as upright as possible. If you cannot do 10 reps, use an assisted dip machine or resistance bands for support.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lowering too deep (past 90 degrees), straining shoulders.
Letting shoulders roll forward and hunch.
Using momentum to push up.
Failing to keep core engaged.

Pose.safety: Keep your core braced and lower back neutral. Avoid arching your back to press heavier weight. Control the barbell throughout.
Pose.Name: Overhead Press (Barbell)
Pose.Equipment: Barbell with Weight Plates, Squat Rack (Optional)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Anterior and Medial Heads), Triceps, Trapezius, Core Stabilizers
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, lower back injury, neck strain

steps.step: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the barbell at your collarbone, hands slightly wider than shoulders, palms forward. Elbows slightly in front of the bar.
Exhale as you press the barbell directly overhead, moving your head slightly back to clear the path. Keep core braced.
At the top, the bar should be directly over your shoulders and ears. Do not hyperextend your lower back.
Inhale as you lower with control back to collarbone. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.
Squeeze your glutes and brace your core throughout the press. This protects your lower back and gives you a stable base.

common_mistakes.mistake: Arching the lower back excessively (leaning back).
Moving the bar around the head instead of moving the head back.
Locking the elbows hyperextended at the top.
Letting the bar drift forward of the shoulders.

Pose.safety: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout. Avoid using momentum to swing the dumbbells. Control the descent.
Pose.Name: Lateral Raise (Dumbbell)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff injury, shoulder impingement, neck strain

steps.step: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides, palms facing your body. Slight bend in elbows, shoulders down.
Exhale as you raise the dumbbells out to your sides, leading with your elbows. Stop when arms are parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly, then inhale as you lower with control back to starting position. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps.
At the top, imagine pouring water from a pitcher - slightly rotate your thumbs down for better medial delt activation.
Do not let your shoulders shrug up. Keep them down and back throughout the movement.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the weights up.
Raising arms above shoulder height.
Shrugging shoulders toward the ears.
Locking elbows straight.

Pose.safety: Keep your chest supported against the bench. Avoid using momentum to swing the dumbbells. Control the movement throughout.
Pose.Name: Rear Delt Fly
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Posterior Head - Rear Shoulder), Rhomboids, Trapezius
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff injury, shoulder impingement, lower back strain

steps.step: Set an incline bench to 30-45 degrees. Lie face down on the bench (chest supported) holding dumbbells with palms facing each other.
Exhale as you raise the dumbbells out to your sides, leading with your elbows. Keep a slight bend in your elbows.
At the top, squeeze your shoulder blades together. Your arms should be parallel to the floor.
Inhale as you lower with control back to starting position. Perform 2 sets of 12 reps.
Focus on moving from your shoulders, not your arms. Imagine squeezing a pencil between your shoulder blades at the top.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the dumbbells.
Rounding the upper back instead of squeezing the shoulder blades.
Raising arms too high (past parallel).
Locking elbows straight.

Pose.safety: Rest your forearms fully on your thighs. Move only your wrists. Control the weight throughout. Avoid jerky movements or using momentum.
Pose.Name: Barbell Wrist Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Wrist tendinitis, carpal tunnel syndrome, elbow tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists hanging over your knees, palms facing up. Hold a barbell with an underhand grip.
Exhale as you curl your wrists upward, bringing the barbell toward your forearms. Keep your forearms pressed firmly against your thighs.
Pause briefly at the top, squeezing your forearm flexors. Do not lift your arms off your thighs.
Inhale as you lower the barbell with control back to the starting position, allowing your wrists to extend fully. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps.
Focus on full range of motion. Let your wrists drop as low as comfortable at the bottom before curling back up. The slower you go, the better the activation.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the entire arm instead of isolating the wrists.
Using momentum to swing the weight up.
Letting wrists hyperextend painfully at the bottom.
Lifting forearms off the thighs during the curl.

Pose.safety: Rest your forearms fully on your thighs. Move only your wrists. Control the weight throughout - do not let it drop quickly.
Pose.Name: Reverse Wrist Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis), wrist tendinitis, carpal tunnel syndrome

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists hanging over your knees, palms facing down. Hold a barbell with an overhand grip.
Exhale as you extend your wrists upward, raising the back of your hands toward the ceiling. Keep your forearms pressed against your thighs.
Pause briefly at the top, feeling the contraction in your forearm extensors (top of the forearm).
Inhale as you lower with control back to the starting position. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.
This exercise is typically weaker than regular wrist curls. Start with an empty barbell or very light dumbbells to master the movement pattern.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the arms instead of isolating the wrists.
Using too much weight, causing jerky movement.
Rushing the descent (eccentric) phase.
Letting wrists drop too low at the bottom.

Pose.safety: Keep your shoulders down and back. Maintain a straight posture. Avoid swinging your body. Walk with control, not speed. Keep your core braced.
Pose.Name: Farmer's Carry
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Forearm Flexors, Grip Muscles, Trapezius, Core Stabilizers, Glutes, Quadriceps, Hamstrings
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Lower back strain, shoulder injury, grip tendinitis

steps.step: Place two dumbbells or kettlebells on the floor shoulder-width apart. Squat down with a flat back, grip the handles firmly, and stand up tall. Keep your shoulders back and core braced.
Begin walking forward with controlled, deliberate steps. Keep your chest up and gaze forward (not down at your feet).
Do not let the weights swing side to side. Keep your core engaged and shoulders down. Breathe steadily.
Walk for 30 seconds, then carefully turn around (using small steps) and walk back to the starting point.
Choose a weight that challenges your grip by the end of 30 seconds but allows you to maintain good posture. If you feel your lower back rounding or shoulders shrugging, the weight is too heavy.

common_mistakes.mistake: Rounding the lower back when picking up the weights.
Letting shoulders shrug up toward the ears.
Swinging the weights side to side while walking.
Walking too fast, losing control of posture.
Holding the breath during the carry.

Pose.safety: Keep your core braced and back straight. Avoid using momentum - control the barbell on both the curl and the descent. Do not arch your lower back.
Pose.Name: Barbell Curl (Heavy)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, lower back herniation, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Elbows pinned to sides, core braced.
Exhale as you curl the barbell toward your shoulders, keeping your upper arms completely stationary. Do not swing your back.
Pause at the top for 1-2 seconds, squeezing your biceps as hard as possible.
Inhale as you lower the barbell with control over 3-4 seconds. Perform 4 sets of 8 reps.
The eccentric (lowering) phase is where muscle growth happens. Lower slowly and deliberately - do not let gravity do the work.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using lower back momentum to lift the weight (cheating curl).
Letting elbows drift forward during the curl.
Dropping the weight quickly on the descent.
Holding breath during the lift.

Pose.safety: Keep your upper arms and back pressed firmly against the bench pad. Do not let your shoulders lift. Control the weight throughout.
Pose.Name: Incline Dumbbell Curl (Advanced)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, shoulder impingement, biceps tear

steps.step: Set bench to 45-60 degrees. Lie back with dumbbells, arms hanging straight, palms facing forward. Press your upper back into the bench.
Exhale as you curl both dumbbells toward your shoulders. Keep elbows stationary and back against the bench.
Pause at the top, squeezing your biceps. Squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Inhale as you lower with control over 3 seconds. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.
For an even deeper stretch, let your arms hang fully extended at the bottom before curling again.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting shoulders lift off the bench.
Using momentum to swing dumbbells up.
Elbows moving forward during the curl.
Rushing the descent phase.

Pose.safety: Keep your upper arm pressed against the inner thigh for stability. Avoid using body momentum. Control the curl and descent fully.
Pose.Name: Concentration Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Spread your legs, hold a dumbbell in one hand. Place your upper arm against your inner thigh, palm facing up.
Exhale as you curl the dumbbell toward your shoulder, keeping your upper arm pressed against your thigh.
Pause at the top, squeezing your biceps as hard as possible.
Inhale as you lower with control to full extension. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps per arm.
This is a pure isolation exercise. Turn your pinky slightly upward at the top for an even better biceps contraction.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using body momentum to swing the weight.
Lifting the upper arm off the thigh.
Rushing the movement.
Not fully extending at the bottom.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides. Avoid leaning back or using body momentum. Control the cable tension throughout.
Pose.Name: Cable Curl
Pose.Equipment: Cable Machine with Bar or Rope Attachment
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Attach a straight bar or rope to a low pulley. Stand facing the cable, holding the attachment with an underhand grip. Elbows pinned to sides.
Exhale as you curl the weight toward your shoulders, keeping upper arms stationary.
Pause at the top, squeezing your biceps. The cable maintains tension even at the top.
Inhale as you lower with control. Do not let the weight stack touch between reps. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps.
The cable provides constant tension throughout the movement, unlike dumbbells where tension decreases at the top and bottom.

common_mistakes.mistake: Leaning back to use body weight.
Letting elbows drift forward.
Allowing weight stack to rest between reps.
Using wrist flexion instead of elbow flexion.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows tucked close to your body. Lower the bar to your lower chest. Control the weight - do not bounce it off your chest.
Pose.Name: Close-Grip Bench Press
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Triceps Brachii (All Heads), Chest (Medial), Anterior Deltoid
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement, pectoral tear

steps.step: Lie on a bench. Grip the barbell with hands shoulder-width apart (closer than regular bench). Lower the bar to your lower chest.
Inhale as you lower the bar with control, keeping elbows tucked close to your body.
Exhale as you press the bar explosively back to the starting position, locking out your elbows.
Perform 4 sets of 8 reps. Keep your feet planted and core braced.
Your elbows should track close to your body - think about bending the bar to engage your lats and protect your shoulders.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward.
Bouncing the bar off the chest.
Arching the lower back excessively.
Using a grip that is too narrow (hands touching).

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pointing toward the ceiling. Avoid flaring elbows outward. Control the weight - do not let it crash down.
Pose.Name: Skull Crushers (Advanced)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Lie on a bench holding an EZ-bar with an overhand grip. Extend arms straight up over your chest.
Inhale as you bend your elbows, lowering the bar toward your forehead. Keep upper arms perpendicular to the floor.
Exhale as you extend your arms back to the starting position, squeezing your triceps.
Perform 3 sets of 10 reps. Lower slowly (3 seconds), press explosively (1 second).
Lower the bar to your forehead, not past your head. The EZ-bar is easier on your wrists than a straight bar.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward.
Lowering the bar past the head (behind the head).
Using momentum to extend the arms.
Arching the lower back.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows tucked close to your head. Avoid flaring elbows outward. Control the cable tension throughout.
Pose.Name: Overhead Cable Extension
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Attach a rope to a low pulley. Stand facing away from the cable, holding the rope behind your head with elbows bent.
Exhale as you extend your arms overhead, keeping elbows pointed forward and tucked close to your head.
At the top, split the rope apart for a better triceps contraction.
Inhale as you return to the starting position with control. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps.
This exercise emphasizes the long head of the triceps, which adds mass to the back of the arm.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward.
Using body momentum to extend.
Not fully extending at the top.
Rushing the return phase.

Pose.safety: Keep your body upright and close to the bars. Lower yourself only to 90 degrees. Avoid shoulder rolling or excessive leaning.
Pose.Name: Tricep Dips (Weighted)
Pose.Equipment: Dip Bars, Weight Belt or Dumbbell between Feet
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Triceps Brachii (All Heads), Chest (Minor), Anterior Deltoid
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, elbow tendinitis, sternum injury

steps.step: Attach a weight belt or hold a dumbbell between your feet. Grip parallel bars, arms straight, shoulders down.
Inhale as you slowly lower your body, keeping elbows tucked and torso upright (triceps focus).
Exhale as you push through your palms to straighten your arms explosively.
Perform 3 sets of 10 reps. Keep your core braced throughout.
For pure triceps focus, keep your body as vertical as possible. Leaning forward shifts emphasis to the chest.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lowering too deep (past 90 degrees).
Letting shoulders roll forward and hunch.
Leaning too far forward (chest dip).
Using momentum to push up.

Pose.safety: Keep your core braced and lower back neutral. Avoid arching your back to press heavier weight. Control the barbell throughout.
Pose.Name: Barbell Overhead Press (Heavy)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Anterior and Medial Heads), Triceps, Trapezius, Core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, lower back herniation

steps.step: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the barbell at your collarbone, hands slightly wider than shoulders. Squeeze glutes and brace core.
Exhale as you press the barbell overhead, moving your head back to clear the bar. Keep your torso rigid.
Lock out at the top with the bar directly over your shoulders and ears.
Inhale as you lower with control back to collarbone. Perform 4 sets of 8 reps.
Squeeze your glutes and quads throughout the press. This creates full-body tension and protects your lower back.

common_mistakes.mistake: Arching the lower back excessively (leaning back).
Moving the bar around the head instead of moving the head.
Letting the bar drift forward.
Not bracing the core before pressing.

Pose.safety: Keep your core braced throughout the rotation. Avoid arching your lower back. Control the dumbbells during the twist.
Pose.Name: Arnold Press
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff injury, elbow tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on a bench with back support. Hold dumbbells at shoulder level, palms facing your body, elbows tucked.
Exhale as you press the dumbbells overhead while simultaneously rotating your palms to face forward.
At the top, palms face forward, arms extended (not locked).
Inhale as you reverse the motion - lower while rotating palms back to starting position. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.
The rotation engages all three deltoid heads. Move slowly and deliberately - this is a technical exercise, not a heavy one.

common_mistakes.mistake: Rushing the rotation.
Arching the lower back.
Using too much weight, losing control of the rotation.
Not fully rotating palms at the top.

Pose.safety: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout. Avoid using momentum. Control the descent - do not let gravity drop the weights.
Pose.Name: Lateral Raise (High Volume)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides, palms facing your body. Slight bend in elbows, shoulders down.
Exhale as you raise the dumbbells out to your sides, leading with your elbows. Stop when arms are parallel to the floor.
Pause for 1 second at the top, feeling the medial deltoid contract.
Inhale as you lower with control over 2-3 seconds. Perform 3 sets of 15 reps.
High reps (15+) work best for lateral raises. Use lighter weight and focus on the burn - not how much you can lift.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the weights up.
Shrugging shoulders toward the ears.
Raising arms above shoulder height.
Dropping the weight quickly on the descent.

Pose.safety: Keep your chest supported against the bench. Avoid using momentum. Control the dumbbells throughout.
Pose.Name: Rear Delt Fly (Advanced)
Pose.Equipment: Dumbbells or Rear Delt Machine, Bench
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Posterior Head), Rhomboids, Trapezius, Rotator Cuff (Infraspinatus/Teres Minor)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff tear, shoulder impingement, neck strain

steps.step: Set an incline bench to 30-45 degrees. Lie face down, chest supported. Hold dumbbells with palms facing each other.
Exhale as you raise the dumbbells out to your sides, leading with your elbows. Keep a slight bend.
At the top, squeeze your shoulder blades together for 1-2 seconds.
Inhale as you lower with control. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps.
Imagine you are trying to squeeze a walnut between your shoulder blades. This mental cue improves rear delt activation.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the weights.
Rounding the upper back instead of squeezing blades together.
Raising arms too high (past parallel).
Locking elbows straight.

Pose.safety: Rest your forearms fully on your thighs. Move only your wrists. Control the weight - do not let it roll out of your hands.
Pose.Name: Barbell Wrist Curl (Advanced)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Wrist tendinitis, carpal tunnel syndrome, elbow tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with wrists hanging over knees, palms up.
Exhale as you curl your wrists upward, bringing the bar toward your forearms. Squeeze at the top.
Inhale as you lower with control, allowing your wrists to extend fully at the bottom.
Perform 3 sets of 15 reps. Do not let the bar roll out of your hands.
For advanced development, add a pause at the top (2 seconds) and bottom (2 seconds) of each rep.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the entire arm instead of just the wrist.
Using momentum to curl the weight.
Not extending fully at the bottom.
Letting wrists hyperextend painfully.

Pose.safety: Rest your forearms fully on your thighs. Move only your wrists. Control the weight throughout - no jerking.
Pose.Name: Reverse Barbell Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis), wrist tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with wrists hanging over knees, palms facing down.
Exhale as you extend your wrists upward, raising the back of your hands toward the ceiling.
Pause at the top, feeling the contraction in your forearm extensors.
Inhale as you lower with control. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.
This exercise builds the top of your forearm and helps with grip endurance for pulling movements.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the arms instead of isolating wrists.
Using too much weight, causing jerky movement.
Not controlling the descent.
Letting wrists drop too low.

Pose.safety: Keep your shoulders down and back. Maintain a straight posture. Avoid swinging your body. Walk with control, not speed.
Pose.Name: Farmer's Carry (Heavy)
Pose.Equipment: Heavy Dumbbells, Kettlebells, or Farmer's Walk Handles
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Forearm Flexors, Grip Muscles, Trapezius, Core Stabilizers, Glutes, Quadriceps
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Lower back herniation, shoulder injury, grip tendinitis

steps.step: Pick up heavy dumbbells or kettlebells from the floor with a flat back. Stand tall, shoulders back, core braced.
Walk forward with controlled, deliberate steps. Keep your chest up and gaze forward.
Do not let the weights swing. Keep your core braced and shoulders down.
Walk for 30 seconds, rest, then repeat. Perform 2 sets of 30 seconds.
This is a full-body grip and core exercise. Go heavy enough that your grip is challenged by 30 seconds, but not so heavy that you cannot maintain posture.

common_mistakes.mistake: Rounding the lower back when picking up weights.
Letting shoulders shrug up toward the ears.
Swinging the weights side to side.
Walking too fast without control.

Pose.safety: This is a heavy strength movement. Use a spotter if possible. Keep your core braced and back straight. Avoid using momentum - strict form is essential.
Pose.Name: Barbell Curl (Heavy - Strict)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, biceps tear, lower back injury, wrist injury

steps.step: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, core braced. Hold the barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Exhale as you curl the barbell toward your shoulders with strict form - no back swing, no momentum.
Pause at the top for 1-2 seconds, squeezing your biceps as hard as possible.
Inhale as you lower with control over 3-4 seconds. Perform 5 sets of 5 reps.
At this weight level, strict form is non-negotiable. If you cannot maintain perfect form, reduce the weight. Quality over ego.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using lower back to generate momentum.
Letting elbows drift forward.
Rushing the descent (eccentric) phase.
Holding breath during heavy lifts.

Pose.safety: Keep your upper arms and back pressed firmly against the preacher pad. Do not lift your elbows off the pad. Control the weight throughout.
Pose.Name: Preacher Curl
Pose.Equipment: EZ-Bar or Dumbbells, Preacher Curl Bench
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, biceps tendinopathy, wrist injury

steps.step: Sit at a preacher curl bench. Place your upper arms flat on the pad, armpits pressed against the top edge. Grip the EZ-bar.
Exhale as you curl the bar toward your shoulders, keeping your upper arms pressed against the pad.
Pause at the top, squeezing your biceps. Do not let elbows lift off the pad.
Inhale as you lower with control to full extension. Perform 4 sets of 8 reps.
The preacher bench removes momentum and isolates the biceps. Do not let the weight stack rest between reps - keep constant tension.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lifting elbows off the preacher pad.
Using momentum to curl the weight.
Not fully extending at the bottom.
Arching the back to lift heavier weight.

Pose.safety: Keep your upper arms stationary throughout. Avoid using momentum. Control the dumbbells on both the curl and descent.
Pose.Name: Incline Dumbbell Curl (Expert)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Biceps Brachii (Long Head - Outer Biceps), Brachialis
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Set bench to 60-70 degrees. Lie back with dumbbells, arms hanging straight, palms facing forward.
Exhale as you curl both dumbbells toward your shoulders. Keep elbows stationary and back pressed into the bench.
As you curl, supinate your wrists (rotate palms up) for maximum biceps contraction.
Inhale as you lower with control over 3-4 seconds. Perform 4 sets of 10 reps.
The steep incline stretches the long head of the biceps more than any other curl variation, promoting muscle growth and separation.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting shoulders lift off the bench.
Elbows moving forward during the curl.
Rushing the descent phase.
Not fully extending at the bottom.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides. This is a drop set - have lighter weights ready. Do not sacrifice form for extra reps.
Pose.Name: Cable Curl (Drop Set)
Pose.Equipment: Cable Machine with Bar Attachment, Multiple Weight Increments
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Biceps Brachii (Constant Tension through Drop Set)
Pose.Weight_Max: 20 kg to 15 kg to 10 kg (Progressive Drop)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, biceps tendinopathy

steps.step: Attach a straight bar to a low pulley. Set up three weight increments: heavy, medium, light.
Perform 12 reps with heavy weight. Do not let the weight stack rest between reps.
Immediately reduce weight to medium. Perform 8 more reps without rest.
Immediately reduce to light weight. Perform 6 more reps. That is one drop set. Rest, repeat for 3 sets.
Drop sets are intense. Perform them at the end of your workout, not the beginning. The goal is muscle fatigue, not injury.

common_mistakes.mistake: Resting between drops (defeats the purpose).
Using poor form on later reps due to fatigue.
Not having weights ready for quick transition.
Performing drop sets at the beginning of the workout.

Pose.safety: Keep your upper arms perpendicular to the floor. Avoid using momentum. Control the dumbbell throughout.
Pose.Name: Spider Curl
Pose.Equipment: EZ-Bar or Dumbbells, Incline Bench (Set at 45 degrees)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, lower back strain

steps.step: Set an incline bench to 45 degrees. Lie face down on the bench, chest supported. Let your arms hang straight down holding an EZ-bar.
Exhale as you curl the bar toward your shoulders, keeping your upper arms stationary.
Pause at the top, squeezing your biceps. Your upper arms should remain perpendicular to the floor.
Inhale as you lower with control to full extension. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps.
The spider curl position prevents body momentum, forcing the biceps to do all the work. You will likely need less weight than standing curls.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting upper arms swing during the curl.
Raising chest off the bench.
Using momentum to lift.
Not fully extending at the bottom.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows tucked close to your body. Use a spotter for heavy sets. Control the bar - do not bounce it off your chest.
Pose.Name: Close-Grip Bench Press (Heavy)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Pectoral tear, shoulder impingement, elbow tendinitis, wrist injury

steps.step: Lie on a bench. Grip the barbell with hands shoulder-width apart. Unrack with spotter assistance.
Inhale as you lower the bar to your lower chest, keeping elbows tucked at 45 degrees.
Exhale as you press the bar explosively back to the starting position, locking out your elbows.
Perform 5 sets of 5 reps. Keep your feet planted and core braced throughout.
At expert level, this is a competition-style lift. Use leg drive, keep your upper back tight, and never bounce the bar off your chest.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward.
Bouncing the bar off the chest.
Lifting hips off the bench.
Not using a spotter for heavy weight.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pointing toward the ceiling. Avoid flaring elbows outward. Control the weight on the descent - do not let it crash down.
Pose.Name: Skull Crushers (Expert)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Lie on a bench holding an EZ-bar with an overhand grip. Extend arms straight up over your chest.
Inhale as you slowly lower the bar toward your forehead over 3-4 seconds.
Exhale as you extend your arms back to the starting position explosively.
Perform 4 sets of 8 reps. Keep upper arms perpendicular to the floor throughout.
For expert development, add a 2-second pause at the bottom (bar just above forehead) before extending. This increases time under tension.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward.
Lowering the bar past the head (behind the head).
Rushing the descent phase.
Arching the lower back excessively.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows tucked close to your head. Avoid flaring elbows outward. Control the dumbbell throughout - do not let it drop.
Pose.Name: Overhead Dumbbell Extension
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Hold a dumbbell with both hands, gripping the inner plates. Extend the dumbbell overhead.
Inhale as you slowly lower the dumbbell behind your head, keeping elbows tucked close to your ears.
Exhale as you extend your arms back to the starting position, squeezing your triceps at the top.
Perform 4 sets of 10 reps. Keep your core braced and lower back neutral.
This is one of the best exercises for the long head of the triceps. Go deep - lower the dumbbell until your forearms touch your biceps.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward.
Arching the lower back to assist the lift.
Not going deep enough in the descent.
Using momentum to extend.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides. This is a drop set - have lighter weights ready. Do not sacrifice form for extra reps.
Pose.Name: Cable Pushdown (Drop Set)
Pose.Equipment: Cable Machine with Rope Attachment, Multiple Weight Increments
Pose.Weight_Max: 30 kg to 20 kg to 15 kg (Progressive Drop)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury

steps.step: Attach a rope to a high pulley. Set up three weight increments: heavy, medium, light.
Perform 12 reps with heavy weight. Keep elbows pinned to sides.
Immediately reduce weight to medium. Perform 8 more reps without rest.
Immediately reduce to light weight. Perform 6 more reps. Perform 3 drop sets total.
At the bottom of each rep, split the rope apart for a deeper triceps contraction. This is a finisher - do it at the end of your triceps workout.

common_mistakes.mistake: Resting between drops (defeats the purpose).
Letting elbows flare outward.
Leaning forward to use body weight.
Not splitting the rope at the bottom.

Pose.safety: Keep your body upright and close to the bars. Lower yourself only to 90 degrees. Control the descent - do not drop into the bottom position.
Pose.Name: Tricep Dips (Weighted - Heavy)
Pose.Equipment: Dip Bars, Heavy Weight Belt or Weight Vest
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, elbow tendinitis, sternum injury

steps.step: Attach a heavy weight belt or wear a weight vest. Grip parallel bars, arms straight, shoulders down.
Inhale as you slowly lower your body over 3-4 seconds, keeping elbows tucked and torso upright.
Exhale as you push explosively back to the starting position, locking out your elbows.
Perform 3 sets of 12 reps. Keep your core braced throughout.
At expert level with heavy weight, every rep must be controlled. If you cannot maintain strict form, reduce the weight.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lowering too deep (past 90 degrees).
Letting shoulders roll forward.
Using momentum to push up.
Leaning too far forward (chest dip).

Pose.safety: This is a heavy strength movement. Use a spotter if possible. Keep your core braced and lower back neutral. Avoid arching your back.
Pose.Name: Barbell Overhead Press (Expert)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Anterior and Medial Heads), Triceps, Trapezius, Core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, lower back herniation, cervical spine issues

steps.step: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, core braced, glutes squeezed. Hold the barbell at your collarbone.
Exhale as you press the barbell overhead with maximum force, moving your head back to clear the bar.
Lock out at the top with the bar directly over your shoulders and ears.
Inhale as you lower with control back to collarbone. Perform 5 sets of 5 reps.
Full-body tension is key at expert level. Squeeze your glutes, quads, and core as hard as you can throughout the entire set.

common_mistakes.mistake: Arching the lower back excessively.
Not bracing the core before pressing.
Letting the bar drift forward of the shoulders.
Rushing the descent phase.

Pose.safety: Keep your core braced throughout the rotation. Avoid arching your lower back. Control the dumbbells during the twist - do not rush.
Pose.Name: Arnold Press (Expert)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear

steps.step: Sit on a bench with back support. Hold dumbbells at shoulder level, palms facing your body, elbows tucked.
Exhale as you press the dumbbells overhead while rotating palms to face forward.
At the top, squeeze your deltoids. Keep the dumbbells close together but not touching.
Inhale as you reverse the motion slowly - lower while rotating palms back. Perform 4 sets of 10 reps.
The Arnold press is a complete shoulder movement. Control the rotation - it is not a race. Each rep should take 4-5 seconds total.

common_mistakes.mistake: Rushing the rotation phase.
Using too much weight, losing control.
Arching the lower back.
Not fully rotating palms at the top.

Pose.safety: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout. Avoid using momentum. Use cable resistance for constant tension.
Pose.Name: Lateral Raise (Cable)
Pose.Equipment: Cable Machine with Handle Attachment, Low Pulley
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Attach a handle to a low pulley. Stand sideways to the cable, holding the handle in the hand farthest from the machine.
Exhale as you raise the cable across your body to shoulder height, leading with your elbow.
Pause at the top for 2 seconds, feeling the medial deltoid contract.
Inhale as you lower with control. Perform 4 sets of 15 reps per side.
Cables provide constant tension throughout the range of motion, unlike dumbbells. Use this to your advantage by never letting the weight stack touch between reps.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the cable.
Raising arm above shoulder height.
Shrugging the shoulder toward the ear.
Letting the weight stack rest between reps.

Pose.safety: Keep your chest supported against the pad. Avoid using momentum. Control the weight throughout.
Pose.Name: Rear Delt Fly (Machine)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Posterior Head), Rhomboids, Trapezius, Rotator Cuff
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff tear, shoulder impingement, neck strain

steps.step: Sit at the rear delt machine with your chest pressed against the pad. Grip the handles with palms facing each other.
Exhale as you pull the handles back in a wide arc, leading with your elbows. Keep a slight bend in your elbows.
At the peak, squeeze your shoulder blades together for 2 seconds.
Inhale as you return to the starting position with control. Perform 4 sets of 12 reps.
Do not let the weight stack touch between reps. Keep constant tension on the rear delts for maximum growth.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the handles.
Rounding the upper back instead of squeezing blades together.
Locking elbows straight.
Letting the weight stack rest between reps.

Pose.safety: Keep your core braced and back straight. Avoid using momentum. Focus on external rotation of the shoulders.
Pose.Name: Face Pull
Pose.Equipment: Cable Machine with Rope Attachment, High Pulley
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Posterior Deltoids, Trapezius, Rhomboids, Rotator Cuff (Infraspinatus/Teres Minor)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement (acute), rotator cuff tear

steps.step: Attach a rope to a high pulley. Stand facing the cable, holding the rope with an overhand grip. Step back to create tension.
Exhale as you pull the rope toward your face, separating the ends. Keep your elbows high (shoulder height).
At the peak, externally rotate your shoulders by pulling the rope ends past your ears. Squeeze your shoulder blades.
Inhale as you return with control. Perform 3 sets of 15 reps.
Face pulls are a prehab exercise. Use moderate weight and focus on external rotation - this builds healthy, resilient shoulders.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using too much weight, compromising form.
Not externally rotating at the peak.
Letting elbows drop below shoulder height.
Using momentum to pull the rope.

Pose.safety: Rest your forearms fully on your thighs. Move only your wrists. Control the weight - do not let it roll out of your hands.
Pose.Name: Barbell Wrist Curl (Expert)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Wrist tendinitis, carpal tunnel syndrome, elbow tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with wrists hanging over knees, palms up.
Exhale as you curl your wrists upward over 2 seconds, squeezing your forearm flexors at the top.
Hold the top position for 2 seconds, squeezing as hard as possible.
Inhale as you lower with control over 3 seconds. Perform 4 sets of 15 reps.
At expert level, add pauses and slow negatives. A 2-second hold at the top and 3-second descent increases time under tension significantly.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the entire arm instead of just the wrist.
Using momentum to curl the weight.
Rushing the descent or eliminating the pause.
Letting wrists hyperextend painfully.

Pose.safety: Rest your forearms fully on your thighs. Move only your wrists. Control the weight throughout.
Pose.Name: Reverse Barbell Curl (Expert)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis), wrist tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with wrists hanging over knees, palms facing down.
Exhale as you extend your wrists upward over 2 seconds, raising the back of your hands toward the ceiling.
Hold the top position for 2 seconds, squeezing your forearm extensors.
Inhale as you lower with control over 3 seconds. Perform 4 sets of 10 reps.
This is often the weaker forearm movement. Do not compare the weight to your regular wrist curl - it will naturally be lower.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the arms instead of isolating wrists.
Using too much weight, causing jerky movement.
Rushing the descent or eliminating the pause.
Not controlling the negative phase.

Pose.safety: Keep your wrists neutral throughout the movement. Control the eccentric (lowering) phase slowly. Avoid using momentum.
Pose.Name: Hammer Curl (Slow Eccentric)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury

steps.step: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides with palms facing each other (neutral grip). Elbows pinned to sides.
Exhale as you curl the dumbbells toward your shoulders over 1 second.
Pause at the top for 1 second, squeezing your brachioradialis.
Inhale as you lower with control over 4-5 seconds. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.
The slow eccentric (lowering) phase builds forearm and grip strength faster than normal tempo. Focus on fighting gravity on the way down.

common_mistakes.mistake: Rushing the descent phase.
Letting elbows drift forward.
Rotating palms during the curl.
Using momentum to curl up.

Pose.safety: Keep your back straight and core braced. Do not let the plates slip. Use chalk if necessary for grip.
Pose.Name: Plate Pinch
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Pinch Grip Muscles (Thumb Flexors, Finger Flexors)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Thumb injury, finger tendinitis, carpal tunnel syndrome

steps.step: Place two weight plates with smooth sides facing outward. Pinch them together with your fingers on one side and thumb on the other.
With a flat back, lift the plates off the floor or rack. Stand tall, arm hanging straight down.
Hold for the prescribed time, squeezing as hard as possible. Do not let the plates slip or rotate.
Perform 3 sets of 45 seconds per hand. Switch hands and repeat.
Pinch strength is crucial for climbing, grappling, and overall hand health. Use two plates of equal size for a stable pinch.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting plates slip or rotate.
Using momentum to lift the plates.
Rounding the back when lifting.
Resting the plates against the body.

Pose.safety: Keep your shoulders engaged and down. Do not swing or kip. Hang with control - do not drop suddenly.
Pose.Name: Dead Hang
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Grip Muscles (Flexors), Forearm Endurance, Latissimus Dorsi (Stretch), Shoulder Stabilizers
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, elbow tendinitis, wrist injury

steps.step: Grip a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart. Hang with arms fully extended, shoulders engaged (not shrugged).
Hang passively, allowing your body to stretch. Keep your core braced to prevent swinging.
Do not let your shoulders shrug up to your ears. Keep them slightly engaged.
Hold for 30-60 seconds. Rest, then repeat. Perform 3 sets.
For advanced grip training, add weight via a dip belt or vest. For variety, alternate grip types (overhand, underhand, mixed, towel).

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting shoulders shrug up to the ears.
Swinging the body back and forth.
Dropping suddenly from the bar (risk of shoulder injury).
Holding breath during the hang.
